english: lish an order; *lon gyur* (*lon gyur*) W. announce me! send in my name!
is said to denote in C. one of the lower classes of officials or noblemen.
1. subst. a bag (not of leather); ras-sgyé (ras sgye) a bag of cotton stuff Pth.; sgyeu (sgyeu) diminutive. - 2. adj. quiet, gentle (of horses) Sp.

a stone for a fire-place, hearth-stone, three of which are so placed together, that a fire may be kindled between them and a kettle put on; sgyíd-bu (sgyid bu) a fire-place constructed in this manner.

pf. and fut. bsgyel (bsgyel), vb. a. to gyél-ba (gyel ba), to throw down, to overthrow, overturn, gan-kyáb (gan kyab) on the back Lex.; to lay or put down (a bottle, a book); to thwart (the charm of an enemy); to kill (horses); *mi se', ta gyel* (*mi se', ta gyel*) manslaughter and the killing of horses, C.

1. a warlike engine to shoot darts or fling stones with, catapult, ballista, sgyógs-kyi (sgyogs kyi) འཕྲུལ་འཁོར་ 'prul-'kór ('phrul 'khor) Thgr. id; sgyogs-rdo (sgyogs rdo) a stone flung from such a machine Sch.; in later times: 2. mortar, cannon, gun, in Tibet even at the present day without wheels, col. *ghyog* (*ghyog*). - 3. a surgeon's instrument for setting broken limbs Cs.

pf. bsgyaṅs (bsgyangs), fut. bsgyaṅ (bsgyang), perh. originally = sgóṅ-ba (sgong ba) to hide, but actually used 1. in C.: *gyú-ma gyáṅ-wa* (*gyu ma gyang wa*) to fill, to stuff (a sausage) 2. col. in W.: *gyáṅ-če* (*gyang ce*) to put into (the pocket, a box, a coffin); *gyáṅ-du bór-če* (*gyang du bor ce*) to keep, lock, or shut up (things); *úgs gyáṅ-če* (*ugs gyang ce*) to hold one's breath; gla pyír sgyóṅ-ba (gla phyir sgyong ba) to retain the wages due to another person Sch. The form rgyaṅs (rgyangs) often occurs in Mil., in passages where 'to retain, lock up, put into' or a similar term would suit very well. Other passages cannot yet be sufficiently accounted for, and upon the whole the roots gyaṅ (gyang) and kyaṅ (kyang) (rgyaṅ (rgyang) etc.) require to be more closely investigated.

W. also *ra* (*ra*), 1. a sound, noise; voice; há-sgra (ha sgra) the sound h Glr.; sgrá-bcas (sgra bcas) འཁྲུ་ k'ru ('khru) noisy evacuations take place Lt.; 'ón-pa-dag sgrá-rnams t'os ('on pa dag sgra rnams thos) the deaf hear sounds; sgra sgróg-pa (sgra sgrog pa) to produce sounds, noises Mil.; sgra dag ysal ysum (sgra dag gsal gsum) (read) loud, correctly, and distinctly, those three (a precept for reading or reciting); *nyíd-ra táṅ-če* (*nyid ra tang ce*) W. to snore; *šóg-ra* (*shog ra*) the noise made by a flight of birds passing; míṅ-sgra (ming sgra) a mere word, name, or sound Was., as a philosophical term. - 2. word, syllable, bdág-sgra (bdag sgra) Cs., bdág-poi sgra (bdag poi sgra) Gram., the name given in grammar to the so-called article pa (pa), e.g. in rtá-pa (rta pa) horseman, rider; dgág-sgra (dgag sgra) prohibitive or negative particle. - 3. language, science of languages, philology. Comp. sgra-skád (sgra skad) (= sgra (sgra) 1.) sound, voice, sgra-skád snyán-pa (sgra skad snyan pa) frq. - sgrá-čan (sgra can) sounding, sonorous. - sgra-čé (sgra che) far-famed, renowned Mil.. sgra čer grays-pa (sgra cher grags pa) Stg. id. - sgra-snyán (sgra snyan) 1. a well-sounding, agreeable voice, 2. C. a guitar. - sgra-brnyán (sgra brnyan) echo Mil. - sgrá-ldan (sgra ldan) sounding, sonorous. - sgra dbyáṅs (sgra dbyangs) 1. pleasing tone, harmony, euphony, e.g. glu daṅ ról-moi (glu dang rol moi) Tar. 2. n. of a goddess Cs. - sgra-sbyór (sgra sbyor) Zam., Tar., Schf., a coalition or connection of letters. - sgra-mí-snyán (sgra mi snyan) (a disagreeable voice) n. of a larger and two smaller northern continents of the fabulous geography of ancient India. - sgra-ts'ád (sgra tshad) (sgra daṅ ts'ad-ma (sgra dang tshad ma)) Tar., Schf.: grammar and logic; yet yi-gei sgra-ts'ád (yi gei sgra tshad), syra-ts'ád-yi-ge (sgra tshad yi ge) Glr. seem to denote philology.

Ssk. राहु Rāhu, 1. a demon or monster of Indian mythology, esp. known by his being at enmity with the Sun and Moon, on whom he is continually wreaking his vengeance, occasionally swallowing them for a time and thereby causing their eclipses. The Buddhist representation of the Rāhu-legend is given by Schl. p. 114 - 2. Cs.: the ascending node of the moon, determining the time of the obscurations. - sgra-ɣcán- (sgra gcan)
